turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I have the opinion of the Court in case 15-1251, National Labor Relations Board versus SW General. Article 2 of the constitution requires that the President obtain the advice and consent of the Senate before appointing officers of the United States. So, what happens when a vacancy arises in an office requiring Presidential Appointment and Senate Confirmation, what is known as a PAS office, and the President and Senate cannot promptly agree on a replacement. Now, since President George Washington's first term in office, Congress has accounted for such a situation by giving the President the authority to appoint acting officials on a temporary basis to perform the functions of the office without first obtaining senate approval. The current version of that authorization is the Federal Vacancies Reform Act. That act authorizes three categories of government officials to become acting officers. The general rule is what you might expect if a vacancy arises, the first assistant steps up and performs the duties of the office in a temporary acting capacity. The President may also however, depart from this default rule and direct a person who is already serving in another PAS office or a senior employee in a relevant agency to become the acting officer instead of the first assistant. Now, the act also makes certain individuals ineligible for temporary acting service. The critical provision in this case states that not withstanding the default rule about first assistant stepping up, a person may not serve as an acting officer if the President nominates that person to fill the vacant PAS office on a permanent basis. Now, it's pretty easy to understand why if a President could put his nominee for an office in as the acting officer, that person could do the job he has been nominated for without ever having been confirmed. Now, the question presented in this case is whether this prohibition on acting service applies only to first assistants who have automatically assumed acting duties under the default rule or whether it also applies to the other people who can serve as acting officers at the President's behest, such as the person from another PAS office or another senior employee in the agency. Now, the facts in this case began in June 2010, when the General Counsel of the National Labor Relations Board resigned. The General Counsel position is a PAS office, and the President directed an individual named Lafe Solomon to serve as acting General Counsel. Solomon qualified for acting service because he had spent 10 years as a senior employee at the board. Now, in January 2011, the President nominated Solomon to serve as General Counsel on a permanent basis. The Senate never took action on the nomination and the President ultimately withdrew Solomon's name in favor of a new candidate whom the Senate confirmed in October of 2013. Now, throughout this entire period however, Solomon continued to serve as acting General Counsel. Now, while serving as acting General Counsel, Solomon pursued an unfair labor practices complaint against respondent, SW General. SW General argued that the complaint Solomon filed was invalid because he became ineligible to serve as acting General Counsel once the President nominated him to fill that role permanently. The Board on the other hand defended Solomon's service. It argued that the prohibition on acting service by nominees applies only to first assistants who automatically step up to perform acting duties under the default rule, not to acting officers who like Solomon, serve under one of the alternatives to that rule. Remember, Solomon was appointed acting General Counsel because he was a senior employee at the agency, not because he was the first assistant. Now why would the Government argue that the rule banning acting service by nominees only applies to some acting officials, those first assistants who stepped up, but not others like Solomon? Well, remember how the rule banning acting service by nominees begins. It begins by saying that it applies notwithstanding the rule allowing first assistants to step up. So, the government says three types of people can become acting officers; first assistants, other PAS officers or senior employees, and the ban on acting service by nominees says it applies notwithstanding the rule allowing first assistants to be acting officers. It does not say anything about the PAS officers or senior employees so the government argues the prohibition does not apply to them. Today, we reject that argument. The text of the act requires that conclusion. By its terms; the prohibition on acting service by nominees applies to any person and bans service under this section. The word “person” is expansive and naturally encompasses anyone who may serve under the act. Simply put, if you are a person, you are covered. Now, if there were any doubt about that, the phrase under this section clarifies that the provision refers to all acting officers since all acting officers are covered by the pertinent section. Well, so, if that is so, why does the notwithstanding clause only mention first assistants and not the other people who can serve and be selected for acting service? Well, we are not sure but it could be because first assistants serve automatically by default while the other officials must be selected in particular cases or because acting service by first assistants is the most common situation. In any event, we think there is no getting around the express language of the prohibition which applies to a person under the section which includes all acting officers, not just first assistants stepping up. There is a thoughtful dissent which I suspect the government will find persuasive, but the ruling of the Court is the other way. The act prohibited Lafe Solomon from continuing his service as acting General Counsel of the NLRB once the President nominated him to fill that position permanently. His decision to file the complaint against SW General was therefore, outside his authority. The judgment of the Court of Appeals below is affirmed. Justice Thomas has filed a concurring opinion. Justice Sotomayor has filled a dissenting opinion which Justice Ginsberg has joined.
